segments.s681-g113: زهر برزنی functions as the subject: زهر (here an individual name or epithet) modified by برزنی. مهتران را is the definite/direct object with the object-marker را placed before the verb for emphasis or topicality. بخواند is a third-person singular past/action verb in Ferdowsi's classical style (the prefixed ب- here appears in older epic usage), meaning “called” or “summoned,” and it completes the clause (SOV order: subject + object را + verb).
segments.s681-g114: به دروازه is a locative phrase introduced by به (‘to/at’), giving the place where the action occurs. بر پاسبانان uses the preposition بر (‘on/upon; in this context ‘before/against’) to relate the position to پاسبانان (the guards). نشاند is the past tense of نشاندن in an archaic/literary sense ‘to seat/place/position’; its logical direct object is the previously mentioned مهتران (marked by را), so the clause means he stationed those chiefs at the gate opposite/in front of the guards.
segments.s681-g105: سپه is the subject 'the army/host'. بازگشت here is a past-tense verbal idea 'returned' (epic style often uses a noun-form as verb), and از پل نهروان is a prepositional phrase 'from the Nahrawan bridge' marking origin. Word order is Subject + Verb + adverbial phrase.
segments.s681-g106: هرآنکس که بودند is a relative/indefinite clause 'whomever they were' / 'each one who was (there)'; بودند is past plural 'they were' referring collectively to the people. پیر و جوان is a simple coordinate phrase 'old and young' describing those people.
segments.s681-g107: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'; چو بهرام برگشت = 'when Bahram returned'. خسرو is the subject for the following action. چوگرد is a connective/adverb ('as/when/at once' in poetic usage) linking the temporal clause to what Khosrow does next.
segments.s681-g108: پل نهروان is the direct object 'the Nahrawan bridge'. سر به سر is an adverbial idiom here meaning 'throughout / from end to end / entirely', and باز کرد is the past action 'opened / reopened' (باز as 'open' + کرد auxiliary 'made/did').
segments.s681-g109: همی‌راند is an epic imperfect/past progressive 'he was riding' (همی- marks continuous aspect), غمگین is the predicative adjective 'sorrowful', and سوی طیسفون is the directional phrase 'toward Tisfōn/Ctesiphon'. The subject is omitted (understood as 'he').
segments.s681-g110: This is a pair of descriptive nominal phrases: دلی پر زغم = 'a heart full of sorrow' (دلی 'heart' + پر زغم 'full of grief') and دیدگان پر زخون = 'eyes full of blood' (دیدگان 'the eyes' + پر زخون 'full of blood/wounds'). The two nouns jointly describe his state.
segments.s681-g111: در شارستانها = 'in the towns/cities' (شارستانها plural). به آهن is instrumental 'with iron' (i.e., with iron bars/locks). ببست is the past verb 'he closed/locked' (ببست = he shut/fastened). Word order: locative phrase + instrument + verb.
segments.s681-g112: بانبوه 'in great numbers' modifies the following noun; اندیشگان 'the thoughtful/wise' is the subject group; درنشست ('in the assembly' / 'at the council') indicates they sat in session — the verb is implied (they sat/assembled).
segments.s681-g57: بزرگان is the nominative plural 'the great ones / nobles' (subject). بنه here means 'bundle / baggage' (not the pistachio) and is the direct object of برنهادند. برنهادند is the past 3rd-person-plural of برنهادن ('to raise / to hoist'); the verb follows the object phrase in this classical word order. وگنج links the following noun phrase (treasure) to the action.
segments.s681-g58: فراوان is an attributive/adverbial 'abundant / plentiful' that relates to the treasure (elliptically carried from the previous segment). ببردن here denotes taking/carrying off (classical poetic form; effectively 'they carried off'), and کشیدند is the past 3rd-person-plural of کشیدن meaning 'they endured / suffered'. رنج is the object 'hardship'. The sequence expresses two coordinated past actions: carrying off much treasure and suffering hardship.
segments.s681-g59: هم آنگه is a temporal linking phrase 'and then / at that moment.' یکی is the indefinite 'one / a certain' introducing the item. اژدهافش is a compound adjective 'dragon‑emblazoned / dragon‑like' modifying درفش, which is the noun 'banner / standard.' Word order is determiner + modifier + noun, typical of classical Persian compounds.
segments.s681-g60: پدید آمد is a two-word verb phrase 'appeared' (پدید + آمد = 'came into view'). و is the coordinating conjunction 'and.' گشت is past 'became' (turning into a new state) and takes the predicate 'گیتی بنفش' — literally 'the world (گیتی) purple (بنفش)', i.e. 'the world became purple.'
segments.s681-g61: پس 'then' is a temporal connector. اندر is a locative/adverbial 'in / among' used with the progressive verb. همی‌راند is an imperfective past 'was driving/was riding' marked by the habitual/continuous prefix همی-. بهرام گرد here is the personal name 'Bahram Gird' (گرد is part of the name rather than a separate adverb), so the whole phrase means 'Bahram Gird was riding/leading on.'
segments.s681-g62: به جنگ is the directional/adverbial phrase 'to/into battle.' از جهان روشنایی is a genitive phrase 'the light of the world' (از marks the source/possession in poetic usage). ببرد is a past third-person form meaning 'he carried / bore / took' (here 'carried into' or 'borne into' battle). The subject (he) is Bahram from the previous segment, so the clause says he bore the world's light into the fight.
segments.s681-g63: رسیدند is the past 3rd-person-plural 'they arrived / they came.' The verb precedes the coordinated subject بهرام و خسرو (a common poetic inversion for emphasis). بهم here means 'to one another / together' indicating they came together or met each other.
segments.s681-g64: This is a string of appositive epithets describing the two men: دلاور 'valiant' (adjective/noun), دو جنگی 'two fighters / warlike ones' (دو numerically specifying the pair), دو شیر 'two lions' (a metaphor for fierce warriors), and دژم is a postpositive adjective 'grim / stern.' The sequence is poetic enumeration: they are brave, warlike, like two grim lions.
segments.s681-g33: همی‌خواست + past verb here gives a prospective/intent meaning: همی‌خواست زد = 'was about to strike' or 'intended to strike.' زد is the past stem of زدن 'to strike' and takes the following prepositional phrase (بر سر شهریار) as its target; بر + سر = 'on/at the head (of...)' with شهریار as the possessor/target (head of the king). Word order is auxiliary/verb then object phrase.
segments.s681-g34: سپر بر سرآورد: سپر 'shield' + بر 'upon' + سرآورد 'brought/raised to the head' — here سرآورد functions as 'lifted/put up (toward the head).' Note the verb precedes its subject (شاه سوار), a common poetic inversion: 'the mounted king' (شاه سوار) is the subject following the verb phrase.
segments.s681-g35: بزیر سپر is a prepositional phrase 'beneath/under the shield.' تیغ is the head noun 'sword,' followed by the post‑modifying compound adjective زهر آبگون 'poison‑coloured / poison‑tinged and bluish' — in classical Persian adjectives commonly follow the noun they modify.
segments.s681-g36: بزد تیغ = 'he struck (with) the sword' — Persian often places the instrument (تیغ) after the verb without an explicit instrumental preposition. و links two verb clauses; انداختش contains the enclitic object -ش 'him/it' (= 'he threw him/it'), and سرنگون is the result complement 'overthrown/cast down.'
segments.s681-g37: خروشید is a past intransitive verb 'he cried/roared.' کای is the archaic vocative particle 'O' calling out to the addressees; نامداران جنگ is the vocative phrase 'renowned ones of war / famed warriors.' The sequence is verb then the quoted vocative/exclamation.
segments.s681-g38: زمانی دگر = 'another while / a little longer' (زمانی 'a time/while' + دگر 'other/another'). کرد here means 'caused/made' (he effected that interval). باید + درنگ expresses necessity: باید درنگ = 'one must pause / a pause is necessary.' The clause states that he (or it) brought about another short delay and notes its necessity.
segments.s681-g39: سپاهش is سپاه + enclitic ش 'his' = 'his army.' همه 'all' emphasizes totality. پشت برگاشتند is an idiom 'turned their backs / retreated' (پشت + برگشتن). The verb برگاشتند is plural, agreeing with the members of the army.
segments.s681-g40: جهانجوی را: را is the definite/object marker marking جهانجوی as the direct object/topic. خوار بگذاشتند is a light-verb predicate: خوار 'despised/humbled' placed before بگذاشتند ('they left/put'), together meaning 'they left him in a state of humiliation' or 'they treated/abandoned the world-seeker as despicable.'
segments.s681-g81: پس = 'then' (narrative connector). اندر = 'into/within' and here gives direction toward the action. همی‌تاخت is the imperfective/classical progressive of تاختن ('to charge, rush') with the prefix همی- marking ongoing or immediate action: 'was charging' / 'charged on'. بهرام is the proper name; تیز is an epithet modifying بهرام meaning 'swift' and follows the name for poetic emphasis.
segments.s681-g82: This noun‑phrase is a pair of ablative/genitive constructions describing Bahram's mental state: سری پر ز کینه = 'a head full of hatred' (سر with the adjective پر 'full (of)' + preposition ز 'of/from' + کینه 'hatred'). دلی پر ستیز = 'a heart full of strife' (دلی here adjectival/relational 'of the heart' or 'heartful' + پر 'full (of)' + ستیز 'strife'). Word order is typical of poetic Persian: adjective/quantifier (پر) follows the noun it qualifies and ز links the filler.
segments.s681-g83: چو here = 'when' (classical subordinating particle). خسرو is the subject (Khosrow). چنان 'so/thus' refers back to the preceding scene. دید is past tense 'saw/observed'. بر پل بماند: بر = 'on/upon', پل = 'bridge', بماند (from ماندن with prefix بـ) is past/habitual here meaning 'he remained/paused' (stopped on the bridge).
segments.s681-g84: جهاندیده is an epithet 'world‑wise/experienced'. گستهم in the line is a proper name (Gostaham, a hero); the provided sense list does not supply this name, so treat it as the personal name here. را is the definite object marker attached to the name. پیش خواند (پیش + خواند) = 'called before/forward' — خواند is past 'he called/summoned' and پیش indicates 'in the presence of/in front'.
segments.s681-g85: بیارید is the imperative plural 'bring!' (classical form corresponding to modern بیاورید). گفتا is the narrative past 'he said' and often follows quoted speech in epic style. کمان مرا = 'my bow' — here 'مرا' is the enclitic possessive/oblique pronoun attached to the noun (an older form where the pronoun and object marker may be segmented), and را is the definite/object marker marking 'کمان' as the thing to be brought.
segments.s681-g86: به indicates motion/tendency 'into/for' and with جنگ اندرون forms the phrase 'into the fighting/inner battle' (اندرون = 'within/inner parts'). ترجمان is 'translator/interpreter' or 'messenger' (here a person to be sent into the fight). Again م + را represent the clitic pronoun + object marker: ترجمان مرا = 'my translator' / 'bring me a translator' depending on context (classical Persian permits this possessive/oblique clitic ordering).
segments.s681-g87: کمانش is 'his bow' (کمان + enclitic ـش 'his'). ببرد (here past/preterite form of بردن with classical vowel pattern) = 'he took/carried off'. آنک = relative 'that which/which', introducing the relative clause. گنجور بود = 'was treasure‑laden / was in the treasury' (گنجور used adjectivally/relationally, بود = 'was'). The clause describes which bow it was.
segments.s681-g88: بران = 'for that' / 'on that account' (classical contraction of بر + آن). کار = 'task/affair/work'. گستهم again is the personal name Gostaham (not the verbal sense listed), so the provided senses don't match the name. دستور بود = 'had the command' or 'was the one in charge' (دستور = command/authority, بود = was). The whole segment says Gostaham was given/held the command for that task.
segments.s681-g17: همی‌گفت is an old/poetic imperfective 'kept saying' (adverbial prefix همی- + بود/گفت form) placed before the subject; هرکس here means 'everyone' (collective use) and is the subject. که introduces the indirect question/quoted clause that follows (literally 'that where Khosrow is'), with خسرو as the proper-name subject and کجا + ست forming the interrogative 'where is (he)'. Word order shows the verb 'ست' at the end of the subordinate clause as in Persian.
segments.s681-g18: The clause beginning with که is an explanatory continuation: 'that today is the day of our victory.' که = 'that'; امروز = 'today'; پیروزی روز = 'day of victory' (پیروزی modifies روز); ما is the possessor 'our' and ست is the copula 'is' placed at the end as usual in Persian poetry.
segments.s681-g19: ببالا 'upward/on high' is fronted as a locative adverbial; همی‌بود is the imperfective copular/continuous 'was standing/was' and follows the adverb before the subject (poetic word order). خسرو is the subject. بدرد is a poetic/elliptical adjective-like use here (literally from the verb 'to cause pain') functioning to mark that he was in pain/wounded — an adjectival/state reading rather than a literal transitive verb.
segments.s681-g20: This is a compact adjectival description: دودیده 'two-eyed/both eyes' modifies an implicit human (Khosrow); پر از خون is a prepositional phrase 'full of blood' describing the eyes; و links the next nominal phrase رخ لاژورد, where رخ 'face' takes the simile/description لاژورد 'lapis-lazuli' (predicative adjective). Note the copula is omitted in the descriptive string, typical of epic diction.
segments.s681-g21: چنین 'thus' is a discourse adverb. تا here functions temporally 'until', and سپیده برآمد 'dawn rose' is the main temporal point; ز کوه 'from the mountain' is a poetic ablative specifying the source of the dawn (common in Persian epic: dawn 'rises' from the mountain). The clause order is typical poetic inversion but reads 'thus until dawn rose from the mountain.'
segments.s681-g22: شد is the past of شدن 'became' (here marking a change/state). از + زخم شمشیر 'from the wounds of the sword' gives the cause/ablative. کشته is a noun 'the slain' (subject), and ستوه is the predicate adjective 'weary'; the clause thus says 'the slain became weary from sword-wounds.' Word order places the verb ahead as a poetic choice but the syntactic relation is verb (شد) + cause (از...) + subject + predicate.
segments.s681-g23: چوشد is a poetic contraction of چو شد 'when it became/when', introducing a temporal clause. دامن تیره شب is a metaphorical phrase 'the dark hem/skirt of night' (دامن modified by تیره and followed by شب), and تا پدید 'until [it] appeared' uses تا as a temporal marker leading to پدید 'appear/emerge' (the coming of light). The clause sets the time when the seeing occurs.
segments.s681-g24: همه 'all/the whole' modifies رزمگه 'battlefield' (here 'the whole battlefield'); کشته و خسته are two coordinated adjectives/nouns 'slain and exhausted' describing those on the field. دید is the main past verb 'saw.' The phrase literally reads 'he saw the whole battlefield (people) slain and weary.'
segments.s681-g73: چو بشنید is a temporal clause 'when he heard' (چو = when; بشنید = ‘he heard’). خسرو is the subject (the king). بگستهم گفت is the main reporting clause 'said b‑…' where بگستهم is an archaic/poetic verb form (here functioning as a verb/phrase attached to گفت) and گفت is the past tense 'said' introducing the reported content.
segments.s681-g74: که introduces the content clause ('that'). با ما کسی نیست is a negative existential: 'there is no one with us' (کسی + نیست = 'no one exists'). در جنگ جفت is a locational/comparative complement: جفت here means 'match/peer' and در جنگ specifies the sphere 'in battle'.
segments.s681-g75: که again introduces reported content ('that'). ما ده تنیم is an archaic way of saying 'we are ten men' (ده تن = ten persons; تنیم = 'we are [ten]'); این سپاهی بزرگ is a nominal phrase 'this great army' apposed or elaborating the preceding clause.
segments.s681-g76: به پیش اندرون is an adpositional phrase meaning 'in the presence of / before the inner [court]': به پیش = 'before, in front of', اندرون = 'inner (quarters)'. پهلوانی سترگ is a descriptive phrase 'a/ of mighty champion(s)': پهلوانی here relates to the champion/heroic sphere, and سترگ is an adjectival epithet (poetic/archaic) meaning 'mighty/ great'. Together they mark the addressee/location of the preceding assertion.
segments.s681-g77: This is a comparative aphorism: هزیمت بهنگام = 'a timely rout' or 'retreat at the right time' (هزیمت = rout/defeat; بهنگام = timely). بهتر زِ جنگ = 'is better than war/battle' (بهتر = better; زـ = 'than' with the following noun جنگ).
segments.s681-g78: چو introduces a conditional/temporal clause 'when/if'. تنها شدی is second‑person past/optative 'you become/are left alone' (شدی = 'you became/you are made'); نیست جای درنگ is an idiomatic negative: 'there is no place/time for delay' (نیست = 'there is not'; جای درنگ = 'room/time for hesitation').
segments.s681-g79: همی‌راند is an imperfect‑continuous poetic form 'was driving/was charging' (همی‑ prefix marks ongoing action). ناکار دیده is an adjectival compound meaning 'untried / inexperienced' (ناکاردیده = literally 'seen as useless / not used to action'), modifying جوان 'young man' (جوان as the head noun).
segments.s681-g80: برین گونه = 'in this manner' or 'thus' (بر + این گونه = 'on/according to this way'). بر تا = directional 'on/toward ... up to' (بر = toward; تا = as far as). پل نهروان is the place‑name 'the Nahrawan bridge' serving as the endpoint of the motion.
segments.s681-g9: This is an address to the second-person plural: شما (you) + verb phrase. بر here is the prefix/particle used with خروشیدن to form برخروشیدن “to burst into a cry / to raise a shout.” خروشید is the 2nd-person plural hortative/imperative-like form meaning “you cry / be in an outcry.” و connects the two commands; اندر + دهید is a light verbal idiom meaning “send/let into / put among,” with دهید as the 2nd-person plural present/imperative of دادن ‘to give/let’. Word order is typical Classical Persian (subject/agent marker — verb — object/phrase).
segments.s681-g10: This clause is the continuation/completion of the command: سران را is the direct object (را marks definiteness). ز introduces the manner/ingredient here (“with / in (blood)”), خون is the noun 'blood', بر + سرافسر is a prepositional/adverbial phrase meaning “all over / everywhere upon,” and نهید is the 2nd-person plural imperative/subjunctive of نهادن ‘to place / set’. The overall order is object — modifying prepositional phrase — verb, typical for emphatic poetic syntax.
segments.s681-g11: بشد is the past/resultative (it became / happened) from شدن used here to report a change of state: “the host became …”. تیز is an adjective meaning “swift/keen” modifying لشکر (army/host). بفرمان is a frozen prepositional phrase “by order / at the command of,” and گو is the verbal root ‘say/speak’ used here as the uttered command (i.e. ‘the order “Go!” was spoken’). Word order places the verb بَشد before its subject complement, a common classical arrangement.
segments.s681-g12: This nominative phrase describes the vanguard: سه ترک (three Turks) is the noun phrase; سر افرازشان is a poetic genitival/participial construction meaning literally “their heads raised” (i.e. ‘with heads held high’), where افراز/افرازشان expresses elevation/height; پیش رو is a compound/adverbial meaning “in front / leading.” The sequence is noun — descriptive phrase — adverb of position.
segments.s681-g13: برلشکر is a fused prepositional phrase meaning “upon the host/army” (بر + لشکر). شهریار is the possessor 'the king' (the army belongs to the king), and آمدند is the 3rd-person plural past tense 'they came/arrived'—here idiomatically 'they attacked' or 'they came against'. The verb is plural and agrees with the implied attackers.
segments.s681-g14: This is a descriptive clause modifying the attackers: جفاپیشه is a compound adjective/noun meaning ‘oppressive/cruel’ (describing their behavior), و links two qualities, کینه (hatred/grudge) + دار functions adjectivally (‘-bearing’/‘full of’) to yield ‘bearing hatred’ or ‘vengeful,’ and آمدند repeats the plural past ‘they came’. In Persian poetic usage the adjective phrases follow or accompany the verb to intensify the description.
segments.s681-g15: خروش آمد is a common poetic sequence: خروش (a roar/clamor) + آمد (came/rose) = “a clamor rose.” از introduces the instruments causing the sound (“from / out of”), followed by a triad of weapon nouns: گرز (mace), گوپال (here listed as a lexical item — treated as a named weapon/term in context), and تیغ (sword). The enumerated nouns after از indicate the source of the noise.
segments.s681-g16: از آهن زمین بود is a verbless or stative clause with بود (past of 'to be') asserting the simile/metaphor 'the earth was of iron' (i.e. the ground rang with iron/was like iron under foot). وز گرد میغ follows as a terser poetic line: وز here introduces the motion/force (literally 'a blast/and'), گرد is 'dust' and میغ is 'blade'—so the phrase describes 'the dust of blades' being blown/whirled about. The verbless, compact structure is typical of epic verse.
segments.s681-g97: یکى (yekī) here is an indefinite subject meaning 'someone' placed before the verb for emphasis. تیر زد is a verbal phrase 'shot an arrow' where زد is the past-tense simple verb 'struck/shot' and تیر is its direct object. بر is a preposition here meaning 'against/at' and بربارگی (a poetic/archaic noun) follows that preposition as the target 'the baggage/burden'. Word order is Subject (indefinite) – Object – Verb with the prepositional phrase marking the target.
segments.s681-g98: بشد is the past/resultive sense 'it happened / it became' introducing the outcome; کار آن باره is a nominal phrase 'the affair (کار) of that occasion (آن باره)' with آن specifying 'that' and باره 'occasion/instance'. یکبارگی is an adverbial/nominalizing word here meaning 'in one instant / at once'. The whole clause states the consequence: 'the affair of that occasion happened/was decided instantly.'
segments.s681-g99: پیاده سپهبد places the adjective/phrase 'on foot' (پیاده) before the title (سپهبد) — 'the spahbed who was on foot' — followed by سپر برگرفت, where برگرفت is the simple past 'took up/raised' and سپر 'shield' is its object. The verb follows the object in this short clause (object–verb order) which is common in this register.
segments.s681-g100: ز بیچارگی is a causal prepositional phrase 'because of/from misery/despair' (ز = 'because of' in poetic register). دست بر سرگرفت is an idiomatic sequence: دست 'hand' + بر (on/to) + سرگرفت. سرگرفت here describes the resulting gesture/state — literally 'he took his hand to his head' or 'he was seized (by grief) and put his hand on his head'. The verb سرگرفت is a past form describing the resulting action/state.
segments.s681-g101: ایلان سینه پیش اندر آمد is a compact clause: یلان 'the heroes' (subject) + سینه پیش 'with chest forward / breast-first' (adverbial phrase describing their posture) + اندر آمد 'came in/advanced' (simple past). چوگرد at the end of the segment is a subordinating particle 'when/as' introducing the following clause. Note the adjective/adverbial phrases precede آمد.
segments.s681-g102: جهانجوی is the noun 'the world-seeker / ambitious one'. کی here functions as the relative/interrogative 'who' introducing a restrictive clause. داشت is the past-tense auxiliary/lexical verb 'had/caused' and او را is the object pronoun 'him' with the object marker را. بمرد is a subjunctive/optative-form verb meaning 'may he die / that he die' used here to express that someone caused the other's death or that he had him killed; syntactically the clause reads 'the world-seeker who had him die / who had him killed.'
segments.s681-g103: هم اندر زمان is a temporal adverbial 'also/at the same time'. اسپ او رابخست has the object-marked pronoun او (his) and رابخست (ra-bakht) as the past verb 'seized/took' with اسپ (horse) as its object; the enclitic را marks اسپ او as definite/object. The clause therefore means 'at that same moment his horse was seized/taken.'
segments.s681-g104: پیاده یلان سینه را پل بجست lists the agents and action: پیاده یلان 'the foot heroes/foot soldiers' (compound noun phrase), سینه را is an adverbial/accusative-like phrase 'with chest (forward)' (here marked by را for emphasis), and پل بجست is a verbal phrase where بجست (past of جستن) is 'leapt / sprang' and پل 'bridge' is the target. The verb follows the object as is common; overall: 'the foot heroes leapt onto the bridge.'
segments.s681-g65: چوپیلان جنگی is a poetic noun phrase meaning ‘war‑elephants’ or ‘fighters like elephants’ (چو = ‘like/as’, here fused with پیلان). برآشوفتند is a past intransitive verb: بر- آشوفتند (بر as a verbal prefix) = ‘were thrown into disorder / became agitated’. Word order places the nominal phrase before the verbal predicate in this line.
segments.s681-g66: همی is an archaic/progressive particle meaning ‘kept on / continued to’; برسریکدگر is a fused prepositional/phrase (بر سر یکدگر) = ‘against one another / upon each other’; کوفتند is the past plural of کوبیدن ‘to strike/beat’, so the clause means ‘they kept striking one another’. The particle همی precedes the action to give continuous/habitual sense.
segments.s681-g67: همی‌گشت is an imperfective/habitual past form meaning ‘kept going / prowled about’. It governs the following proper name بهرام as its subject. چون شیر نر is a simile ‘like a male lion’ modifying Bahram’s movement.
segments.s681-g68: سلیحش is سلیح + ـش (his weapon). نیامد here is idiomatic: ‘did not come’ → ‘did not prove useful / did not avail’. برو کارگر functions as a small prepositional/adjectival phrase meaning ‘to/for the work’ → ‘effective / of use’; together the clause says his weapon did not prove effective. (برو is an archaic linker ‘for / to’ here, and کارگر = ‘effective’.)
segments.s681-g69: برین گونه = بر + ین + گونه = ‘in this manner / thus’ (برین = بر این). تا introduces a temporal clause ‘until’; خور is the sun and ز گنبد means ‘from the dome / vault (of heaven)’. بگشت is a past verb meaning ‘turned / moved’ (here: the sun turning from the dome). The whole clause gives a temporal frame: ‘in this way until the sun turned from the vault.’
segments.s681-g70: از اندازه = ‘beyond measure / from the measure’; آویزش is the hanging/pendant (آویزش = that which hangs); اندر گذشت is a past construction ‘passed within / went beyond’ — together: ‘its hanging passed beyond measure’ (i.e. exceeded the proper bounds). اندر is an archaic locative particle meaning ‘in/within’.
segments.s681-g71: تخوار here functions as a proper name (a messenger), though the provided lexical senses do not list a name form (so marked below as not matching). آن زمان = ‘at that time’; پیش خسرو رسید = ‘reached before Khosrow / arrived in Khosrow’s presence’ (پیش + person = ‘in the presence of’). The verb رسید is simple past ‘arrived’.
segments.s681-g72: که is the subordinating/complementizer ‘that’ introducing reported content. گنج وبنه = ‘treasure and baggage/belp (stores)’ (وبنه here is a material/baggage word not matched in the given sense list). زان سوی پل = ‘to/from that side of the bridge’ (زان = ‘that’ + سوی = ‘side/direction’, so ‘the other side of the bridge’). کشید is past tense ‘drew / pulled / carried to’ (the intended sense is ‘had been drawn/moved’), but the exact sense is not among the provided options and is marked below as not matched.
segments.s681-g89: کمان (object) appears before the verb in a typical poetic SOV order; بر گرفت is the verb complex where بر is a verbal prefix meaning roughly “up / on” and گرفت (past of گرفتن) means “took / seized.” آن is the demonstrative “that” placed before the noun سپهدار (the subject “commander”). گرد here follows the noun as a postpositive epithet or modifier (in epic Persian such short words often function as a descriptive epithet or name element), so the clause reads literally “That commander (گرد) took up the bow.”
segments.s681-g90: بتیر is a fused prepositional/instrumental phrase (به تیر → “with/by an arrow”). از هوا روشنایی is a noun phrase where از هوا (“from the air”) qualifies روشنایی (“the brightness/light”) as its source. ببرد is the past/punctual form of بردن (here “to take away / carry off”); the structure means “by an arrow he carried off the brightness from the air.”
segments.s681-g91: همی is the habitual/progressive particle used in epic Persian to mark ongoing or repeated action (here ‘kept on’ / simple vivid past). تیر is the subject and بارید is the past tense verb “rained / fell,” while همچون تگرگ is a simile ‘like hail.’ The clause reads naturally as “arrows rained like hail.”
segments.s681-g92: بیک here functions as a prepositional-like unit (classically be-yek = “to/onto one”) and governs چوبه (“a beam / frame”), so بیک چوبه = “onto a single beam.” با سر is an instrumental phrase ‘with (their) heads’; همی‌دوخت is the imperfective verb from دوختن (“was sewing / fastened”), used metaphorically to mean the arrows’ heads were driven/fastened into the beam. ترگ is the direct object (literally “rags / shreds”), producing a vivid simile: the arrows’ heads fastened like sewing rags to the beam. Word order is again object/adjunct + verb.
segments.s681-g93: پس is a temporal/consecutive adverb “then.” اندر marks location/entry (“into / in”), and همی‌تاخت is the progressive past of تاختن (“to charge / rush”), so اندر همی‌تاخت = “was charging in.” بهرام شیر is the subject in apposition (name + epithet “the lion”); the subject follows the verb as often in Persian epic narrative.
segments.s681-g94: کمندی بدست is an instrumental phrase: کمندی (“a lasso / snare”) followed by بدست, here meaning “in (his) hand.” اژدهایی is the object “a dragon” (indefinite -i ending), and بزیر is an adverbial/resultative “down / to the ground.” The clause implies the action of bringing the dragon down by means of the lasso; the main verb is understood from the previous clause (he charged and (thereby) brought the dragon down).
segments.s681-g95: چوخسرو is the conjunction چو (“when”) fused to the name خسرو, so it functions as a temporal subordinate “when Khosrow …”. The small particle/letter و here is an archaic emphatic/object pronoun form (classical usage often writes او or و for ‘him’), followed by را marking the definite object. دید is the past tense “saw,” and برگشت شاد is a consecutive clause “he returned / withdrew, glad.” The sequence therefore reads “When Khosrow saw him, he turned back, joyful.”
segments.s681-g96: دو زاغ is a noun phrase “two zāgh” (zāgh = rook/crow); کمان را is the definite object “the bow” (را marks it). برنهاد is the past of برنهادن (برنهاد) meaning “placed / set down / raised up” depending on context; here it means they put the bow down or laid it aside. The clause therefore describes that “two zāgh laid down / set down the bow,” a concise, image-driven statement typical of epic diction.
segments.s681-g41: به introduces the indirect object ('to/for'); here it marks the persons being addressed. The names بندوی و گستهم (Bandoy and Gostaham) follow the preposition and are linked by و 'and'. گفت is the simple past third-person 'said'; آن زمان is an adverbial time phrase ('at that time/then') placed after the verb for emphasis (poetic/inverted order). The subject is omitted (understood from context).
segments.s681-g42: که introduces a subordinate (content) clause 'that/which' here introducing what was said. اکنون شدم uses اکنون 'now' + شدم (past of شدن) meaning 'I have become' (result state). زین سخن is an older/poetic way to say 'of/from this speech' (زین = 'this/of this' + سخن 'speech'), and بدگمان is a predicative adjective 'suspicious/doubtful' describing the speaker's state after شدن ('become suspicious').
segments.s681-g43: رسیده is a past participle/adjective 'arrived/ripe/grown' (here 'grown/come of age'); مرا (written here in clipped form) is the dative/possessive 'to me / for me' (indicating whose offspring is in question). هیچ فرزند نیست is a negative existential: هیچ 'no/none' + فرزند 'child/descendant' + نیست 'does not exist/is not', so the whole clause says 'no (grown) child has come to me' or 'I have no son (of age)'.
segments.s681-g44: همان here means 'the same / likewise' connecting this clause to the previous thought. از در تاج literally 'from the door/house of the crown' (در = gate/house, تاج = crown/sovereignty) — an idiom for the royal line/house. پیوند نیست: پیوند 'bond/link' + نیست 'does not exist' = 'there is no (line of) succession/tie to the crown.' Word order places the locative/source phrase (از در تاج) between same/subject and predicate for poetic effect.
segments.s681-g45: اگر introduces a conditional clause ('if'). من شوم کشته combines the subjunctive/present-tense form شوم of شدن with the past participle کشته to form a passive/irrealis sense 'if I am (were to be) slain'. در کارزار is an adverbial prepositional phrase 'in battle' specifying where the action occurs.
segments.s681-g46: جهان را uses را as a topical/emphatic marker ('as for the world'); نماند is the negative of ماندن 'remain' and here functions as 'will not remain / will be left' (future/resultive sense). یکی شهریار places the numeral/indefinite one before the noun: 'a single sovereign / one king.' The clause therefore asserts that not even one ruler will remain in the world if the speaker is slain.
segments.s681-g47: بدو is the dative/enclitic form 'to him/unto him' (prepositional/oblique 'to him'), followed by گفت 'said'. بندوی is the speaker's name (Bandoy). کای is the vocative particle 'O' (classical/epic address), and سرفراز is an epithet 'noble/exalted/victorious' used in direct address. The whole segment is the speech-introducing formula 'Bandoy said to him, O exalted one.'
segments.s681-g48: بدین روز is a frozen expression 'on this day' (بدین = به + این, 'to/at this'); هرگز مبادت is an optative/negative jussive 'may it never be' (مبادت = 'may it not happen' — archaic/poetic wish formula). نیاز is the noun 'need/poverty/necessity' serving as the thing being wished away: 'may need never befall you on this day.' Word order places the time phrase first for emphasis.
segments.s681-g49: سپه رفت اکنون is a simple past statement: 'سپَه' (the army/host) is the subject and 'رفت' is the past-tense verb 'went/left', with 'اکنون' ('now') as a temporal adverb. The clause تو ایدر مه ایست follows as a predicative phrase: 'تو' is the pronoun 'you', 'ایدر' is the locative adverb 'here', and 'مه ایست' is a predicate with 'ایست' = 'stands/remains' — 'مه' is used poetically (see senses) to qualify the subject. Word order is typical Persian: topical/temporal adverb + subject + verb, then a separate verbless/nominal predicate about 'you'.
segments.s681-g50: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause 'that/for which'. کس here is negated by the verb 'نیست' and therefore functions as 'no one'. در زمانه is a locative phrase 'in the age/era', and تو را uses the object/topic marker 'را' with 'تو' to signal the person affected (literally 'for you'). یار نیست is an existential/negative copular construction meaning 'there is no friend/ally'. Overall: 'that no one in the age is your ally.'
segments.s681-g51: بزنگوی is the instrument/agent ('buzangui' — bell or trumpet sound) and functions as the speaker here. گفت is the past verb 'said/told' (third person past). آن زمان is a temporal adverbial 'at that time/then' modifying the saying, and شهریار is the addressee 'the king' (Persian often places the indirect object after the verb without a preposition).
segments.s681-g52: کز here introduces the content/reason clause and functions like 'that/from which' (essentially 'from here' in context). ایدر = 'here', and برو is the imperative 'go/leave'. تازیان تاتخوار is a nominal phrase giving the reason or danger: 'the Taziān (Arabs) — Tāt‑khwār' (an epithet meaning roughly 'devourers/raiders'); it is a verbless clause linked to the imperative (i.e. 'leave, for the Arabs are ...').
segments.s681-g53: ازین ماندگان = 'from these/from those remaining': از = 'from/out of', ین is the orthographic variant of 'این' ('these'), and ماندگان is the plural substantive 'the remaining/left‑behind ones'. بر سواری هزار places the numeral after the unit: 'a thousand on horseback' / 'a thousand horsemen', where بر + سواری indicates 'mounted (on horse)'. The whole segment names the number and type of men to be taken from the survivors.
segments.s681-g54: بران here functions adverbially/directional ('to there' / 'thence') directing the action to the battlefield (رزمگاه). آنچ = 'that which/whatever' introduces an indefinite relative, یابی is a form of یابیدن used here with an imperative/subjunctive sense 'you may find' or 'you find', and بیار is the imperative 'bring'. The sequence means 'send them to the battlefield and bring whatever you find (there)'.
segments.s681-g55: سراپرده is a locational/nominal head 'the inner chamber/behind the curtain' (setting the place), followed by a list of contents: دیبه (diabeh) = fine silk or brocade, وگنج = 'and treasure', و تاج = 'and crown'. The conjunction و attaches the listed items; the clause is a verbless inventory of what the tent contained.
segments.s681-g56: همان = 'the same' / 'those same' continues the enumeration. بدره literally 'full moon' is a poetic epithet often used for beautiful (moon‑like) faces; وبرده here is 'and slaves/captives' (برده = taken/enslaved), and وتخت عاج is a compound 'and (an) ivory throne' (تخت = throne, عاج = ivory). The sequence continues the inventory with poetic imagery and compounding (تخت عاج).
segments.s681-g25: بگردنکشان is ب + گردنکشان, a prepositional/benefactive phrase meaning 'to the proud/haughty ones' (b- marks the indirect object or addressee). گفت is the simple past of گفتن 'said' used here as a reporting verb. یاری کنید is an imperative (second-person plural) — یاری (noun 'help') + کنید (imperative of کردن 'do/make') — so literally 'Do help' = 'Give help!' or 'Help!'.
segments.s681-g26: برین is orthographic contraction of بر + این (بر این = 'against/on these' or 'upon these'); here بر marks opposition/target ('against these'). دشمنان is the plural noun 'enemies'. کامگاری کنید is a compound where کامگاری ('success, obtaining what is desired') is the object and کنید is the plural imperative 'achieve/do' — 'Achieve success (over) these enemies.'
segments.s681-g27: که is the subordinating/relative conjunction 'that/for/which' introducing the clause. پیروزگر is a substantive 'the victor/conqueror'. پشت here is a noun meaning 'support' or 'backing' (not a spatial preposition). و is the coordinating 'and'. یارمنست is an archaic/poetic contraction of یار من است ('he is my friend/companion') — noun + possessive + copula fused into one word.
segments.s681-g28: همان is a demonstrative 'that/the same' pointing back to the antecedent. زخم شمشیر is a genitive/nominal compound 'wound of the sword' (زخم modified by شمشیر). کارمنست is another fused poetic form: کارِ من است ('it is my work/deed') — کار (task/deed) + من (my) + است (copula), here asserting that the wound is the speaker's deed.
segments.s681-g29: بیامد is the simple past 'came/arrived.' دمان (دمان) is a noun ('hem/skirt' or a marginal band) used here as the subject or moving element. تا functions as a prepositional/limit marker 'up to/as far as.' بر آن سه ترک is a prepositional phrase 'upon/against those three Turks' (بر = on/against, آن = 'those', سه ترک = 'three Turks').
segments.s681-g30: This segment lists groups/nouns with modifiers in the usual Persian order: numeral (نه 'nine', present in the surface) + noun (ترک) + adjective (دلاور 'brave') yielding 'nine brave Turks', followed by numeral + noun (سه پیل) + adjective (سترگ) 'three (very) large elephants'. Adjectives follow the nouns they modify here, and numerals precede the noun.
segments.s681-g31: یکی is an indefinite 'one' / 'someone' introducing an agent. تاخت is the past tense 'charged/galloped' (a sudden forward movement). تا here is the conjunction 'until' marking the extent of motion, and نزد خسرو رسید is a verb phrase 'reached Khosrow' (نزد = 'near/in the presence of', خسرو = the king's name, رسید = arrived/reached). The sequence shows motion verb + hasta-clause + result 'he rode until he reached Khosrow.'
segments.s681-g32: پرنداوری is a nominal phrase 'the fluttering/flight of wings' used as the direct object. از میان is a prepositional expression 'out of/from the midst (of them)' (از = 'from', میان = 'middle/midst'). برکشید is past tense of برکشیدن and here takes the sense 'drew up/drew forth' (commonly used of drawing a weapon) — 'he drew (it) out from among (them)'.
segments.s681-g1: ز (preposition) introduces the source 'from the host' (ز + لشکر). گزین کرد is a compound past tense: گزین (choose) + کرد (do/make) = 'chose/selected'. بَهرام شیر is the subject placed after the verb; شیر is an epithet apposed to the name ("Bahram the Lion").
segments.s681-g2: سپاهی is the noun 'troops/warriors' that functions as the thing selected (object of گزین کرد). جهانگیر وگرد دلیر are adjectival/epithetic modifiers: جهانگیر = 'world‑conquering', وگرد = 'throng/host' used here as intensifying epithet, and دلیر = 'brave'.
segments.s681-g3: چوکردند is a fused clause meaning 'when they did so' (چو = when + کردند = they did). و is the coordinating 'and'. با او دبیران marks participants/instrumental accompaniment ('with him, the secretaries'); شمار ('count'/'tally') is the counting activity and is tied to the verb کرد(ند) earlier (i.e. 'they made/counts').
segments.s681-g4: سپه is the subject 'the army/host'. بود is the past copula 'was' (links subject to predicate). شمشیر زن is a compound meaning 'sword‑strikers' → 'swordsmen' (شمشیر = sword, زن here = one who strikes), and صد هزار is the numeric quantifier 'one hundred thousand'.
segments.s681-g5: ز introduces origin/partitive 'from'. خاقانیان is a plural noun ('the Khaghan's people' / imperial retinue). آن سه ترک specifies 'those three Turks' (آن = that/those, سه ترک = three Turks). سترگ in classical Persian is an archaic epithet (here meaning 'mighty/great'); none of the provided glosses fit exactly, so it is treated as irregular/archaic here.
segments.s681-g6: که is the relative pronoun 'who/that'. بودند is the past plural of 'to be' ('they were'). غرنده is an adjective 'roaring/howling'. برسان here functions poetically to make a simile/compare ("like/as a..."), and گرگ is the simile's vehicle 'wolf' — together 'roaring like wolves'. The precise grammatical force of برسان is idiomatic/poetic, so it is marked as irregular relative to the supplied senses.
segments.s681-g7: به جنگ‌آوران گفت — به marks the indirect object 'to the warriors'; جنگ‌آوران is the addressee plural 'warriors'. گفت (past of گفتن) is 'he said'; in the list given the usual 'to say' sense is not present, so its dictionary senses don't exactly match here. چون introduces the temporal clause 'when/as'. زخم کوس is a poetic phrase: زخم 'blow/strike' (here of a drum) + کوس 'war‑drum' = 'the drum‑stroke'.
segments.s681-g8: برآید is a present/subjunctive-like or jussive form 'arises/is sounded' used here with temporal force. بهنگام is an adverbial phrase 'at the time of/at (the moment of)'. بانگ is 'cry/sound' and خروس is 'rooster' — together 'at the rooster's cry' = 'at cockcrow'.

sentences.s681-s57: Zahr (the one of the plain) summoned the chiefs and set them at the gate before the guards.
sentences.s681-s53: The army returned from the Nahrawan bridge — every one of them, old and young.
sentences.s681-s54: When Bahram returned, Khosrow at once reopened the Nahrawan bridge from end to end.
sentences.s681-s55: He rode sorrowfully toward Ctesiphon, with a heart full of grief and eyes full of blood.
sentences.s681-s56: He fastened the city gates with iron; in great numbers the thoughtful assembled at the council.
sentences.s681-s29: The great men shouldered their packs, and, carrying off abundant treasure, they endured hardship.
sentences.s681-s30: Then a dragon-emblazoned banner appeared, and the world turned purple.
sentences.s681-s31: Then Bahram Gird was riding about, and he carried the world's light into battle.
sentences.s681-s32: Bahram and Khosrow met — two valiant fighters, two grim lions.
sentences.s681-s17: He was about to strike at the king's head; the mounted king raised his shield.
sentences.s681-s18: Beneath the shield, a bluish, poison‑tinged sword; he struck with the sword and cast him down.
sentences.s681-s19: He cried out, 'O renowned warriors of war!' — he caused (or allowed) another short interval; a pause was necessary.
sentences.s681-s20: His whole army turned their backs; they left the ambitious man humbled.
sentences.s681-s41: Then Bahram, swift, charged in — his head full of hatred, his heart full of strife.
sentences.s681-s42: When Khosrow saw this he stayed on the bridge and summoned the world‑wise Gostaham before him.
sentences.s681-s43: He cried, 'Bring it!' and said, 'My bow — bring it; bring me a messenger/interpreter into the fighting.'
sentences.s681-s44: He took his bow — the one that was in the treasury; for that task Gostaham had the command.
sentences.s681-s9: Everyone kept saying, "Where is Khosrow? — for today is the day of our victory."
sentences.s681-s10: High above stood Khosrow, wounded — his two eyes full of blood and his face like lapis-lazuli.
sentences.s681-s11: Thus, until dawn rose from the mountain, the slain became weary from sword-wounds.
sentences.s681-s12: When the dark hem of night withdrew and the dawn appeared, he saw the whole battlefield slain and exhausted.
sentences.s681-s37: When Khosrow heard this, he said, “There is no one among us who is a match in battle.”
sentences.s681-s38: that we are ten men — this great army — before the inner court's mighty champion.
sentences.s681-s39: A timely rout is better than battle; when you become alone there is no place for delay.
sentences.s681-s40: Thus the inexperienced young man kept charging, and in this manner pushed on up to the Nahrawan bridge.
sentences.s681-s5: You raise a cry and send it in; place the chiefs' heads everywhere, stained with blood.
sentences.s681-s6: The host became swift at the command “Go”; three Turks, with heads raised, marched in front.
sentences.s681-s7: They fell upon the king's host; they came cruel and full of hatred.
sentences.s681-s8: A clamor rose from maces, Gopāl and swords; the earth was of iron, and the blades' dust whirled about.
sentences.s681-s49: Someone shot an arrow at the baggage; the matter of that episode was settled at once.
sentences.s681-s50: The spahbed on foot took up his shield; overwhelmed by despair, he put his hand to his head.
sentences.s681-s51: The heroes came forward chest-first; when the ambitious world-seeker — who had him killed — appeared.
sentences.s681-s52: At the same time his horse was seized; the foot heroes sprang onto the bridge.
sentences.s681-s33: The war-elephants were thrown into confusion, and they kept striking one another.
sentences.s681-s34: Bahram prowled like a male lion; his weapon proved of no use.
sentences.s681-s35: In this manner, until the sun turned from the vault of heaven, its hanging surpassed all measure.
sentences.s681-s36: At that time Takhvâr reached Khosrow and said that the treasure and baggage had been drawn to the far side of the bridge.
sentences.s681-s45: That commander took up the bow; with an arrow he robbed the air of its brightness.
sentences.s681-s46: Arrows rained down like hail; with their heads they were fastened to a single beam as if sewing on shreds.
sentences.s681-s47: Then Bahram the Lion charged in; with a lasso in hand he brought a dragon down.
sentences.s681-s48: When Khosrow saw him, he turned back joyful; two rooks (two loud cries/omens) laid down the bow.
sentences.s681-s21: At that time he said to Bandoy and Gostaham, 'Now I have become suspicious of this speech.'
sentences.s681-s22: No grown son has come to me; likewise there is no link of succession to (the) crown.
sentences.s681-s23: If I am slain in battle, there will not remain a single sovereign in the world.
sentences.s681-s24: To him Bandoy said: 'O exalted one, may you never be in need on this day.'
sentences.s681-s25: The army has departed; now you stand here — there is no one in this age who is your ally.
sentences.s681-s26: The buzangui (bell/trumpet) told the king then: 'Go away from here — the Arabs are pillagers (Tāt‑khwār).'
sentences.s681-s27: From those left behind, mount a thousand horsemen; send them to that battlefield, and bring whatever you find.
sentences.s681-s28: In the inner tent were brocades, treasure, and a crown; likewise moon‑like beauties, captives, and an ivory throne.
sentences.s681-s13: He cried to the proud ones, "Give aid; win success against these enemies."
sentences.s681-s14: For the victor is my support and companion; that wound of the sword is my doing.
sentences.s681-s15: It came up to those three Turks — nine brave Turks and three mighty elephants.
sentences.s681-s16: One charged; he rode until he reached Khosrow, and drew (his weapon) from among them.
sentences.s681-s1: From the host Bahram the Lion selected an elite company of warriors, world‑conquering and brave.
sentences.s681-s2: When they had done so, and the secretaries counted with him, the army numbered a hundred thousand swordsmen.
sentences.s681-s3: From the Khaghan's retinue were those three mighty Turks, who howled like wolves.
sentences.s681-s4: He told the warriors: when the drum's stroke is sounded at the cock's cry (i.e. at cockcrow).
